title: US state AG AI enforcement tracker
description: State attorney general AI enforcement actions with dates, penalties, legal authorities, statuses, and primary sources, plus open investigations and enforcement-readiness materials. Covers biometric AI settlements, algorithmic rent pricing, AI voice-clone robocalls, deceptive AI claims, and AI lending discrimination. Not legal advice; confirm against the linked primary sources.
citation: Vorp Labs, US State AG AI Enforcement Tracker (as of 2026-07-18), https://vorplabs.com/ai-regulatory-updates/state-ag-enforcement

actions.agOffice: Nine state attorney general offices, in United States v. RealPage
actions.legalAuthority: Sherman Act Sections 1 and 2 plus state antitrust and consumer-protection claims in the amended RealPage complaint
actions.statusDetail: Settlement filed 2025-11-18; the DOJ RealPage case index lists the Greystar final judgment as entered 2026-03-02.
actions.outcome: $7,000,000 aggregate payment to the nine participating states; restrictions on revenue-management products that use competitors' nonpublic, competitively sensitive data; cooperation in the continuing RealPage case.
actions.summary: Nine state attorneys general settled their claims against Greystar in the joint federal and state RealPage action, alleging Greystar used RealPage's shared algorithmic rent-setting system to align rents rather than pricing independently. The settlement is a state disposition distinct from the federal proposed judgments in the same docket.
actions.aiIssue: Algorithmic rent pricing with competitors' nonpublic data

penalties.note: Aggregate multistate amount, counted once rather than once per state.

federalParallels.label: DOJ and plaintiff states v. RealPage on the federal tracker

factChecks.label: Settlement amount
factChecks.value: Colorado announced a $7 million multistate settlement with Greystar dated November 18, 2025.

factChecks.label: Final judgment
factChecks.value: The DOJ RealPage case document index lists Final Judgment [Greystar Management Services, LLC] dated March 2, 2026, the first final judgment in the case.

factChecks.label: Massachusetts allocation
factChecks.value: Massachusetts reported receiving $621,988 from the multistate Greystar settlement.

openItems: mass.gov blocks automated clients (HTTP 403); the Massachusetts release URL was verified via an archived snapshot.

actions.agOffice: Colorado and North Carolina attorney general offices
actions.shortName: Colorado and North Carolina v. Cortland (RealPage action)
actions.legalAuthority: Sherman Act and state antitrust claims arising from the RealPage action
actions.statusDetail: Agreement announced 2025-04-11; claims against other defendants in the RealPage action continued.
actions.outcome: Cortland agreed not to use nonpublic competitor data through RealPage rent-pricing software and to cooperate in the continuing case. No monetary state payment was reported in the Colorado release.
actions.summary: Colorado and North Carolina reached an agreement with Cortland resolving state claims that Cortland participated in rent-setting through RealPage's algorithm-driven system using landlords' private rental data.
actions.aiIssue: Algorithmic rent pricing with competitors' nonpublic data

federalParallels.label: DOJ and plaintiff states v. RealPage on the federal tracker

factChecks.label: Agreement terms
factChecks.value: The Colorado AG announced on April 11, 2025 that Cortland agreed to stop using illegal rent-pricing software features based on nonpublic competitor data and to cooperate in the case.

actions.agOffice: Arizona Office of the Attorney General
actions.target: RealPage, Inc. and nine residential landlords
actions.legalAuthority: Arizona Uniform State Antitrust Act; Arizona Consumer Fraud Act
actions.statusDetail: Standalone Arizona lawsuit announced 2024-02-28. Arizona is not listed among the plaintiff states in the joint DOJ RealPage case.
actions.outcome: Injunctive and monetary relief sought; no resolution verified as of the last check.
actions.summary: Arizona sued RealPage and nine major residential landlords operating in Arizona, alleging they conspired to raise rents by replacing independent pricing decisions with software-generated recommendations built on competitors' confidential pricing and occupancy data.
actions.aiIssue: Algorithmic rent pricing with competitors' nonpublic data

federalParallels.label: DOJ and plaintiff states v. RealPage on the federal tracker

factChecks.label: Filing
factChecks.value: The Arizona AG announced the lawsuit against RealPage and nine landlords on February 28, 2024, citing the Arizona Uniform State Antitrust Act and the Arizona Consumer Fraud Act.

openItems: Maricopa County Superior Court docket status and the complete defendant roster have not been verified from court records.

actions.agOffice: Massachusetts Office of the Attorney General
actions.legalAuthority: Massachusetts Consumer Protection Act, M.G.L. c. 93A, and fair-lending laws cited by the AGO
actions.statusDetail: Settled through an assurance of discontinuance filed in Suffolk Superior Court; compliance and reporting obligations remain ongoing.
actions.outcome: $2,500,000 payment to Massachusetts; AI governance and compliance policies; ongoing reporting to the AGO; discontinuation of the Cohort Default Rate variable and an immigration-status Knockout Rule in underwriting models.
actions.summary: The Massachusetts AGO alleged that Earnest's AI and algorithmic student-loan underwriting models created unmitigated risks of disparate harm to Black, Hispanic, and non-citizen applicants and borrowers. The assurance of discontinuance imposes model-variable, governance, and reporting remedies alongside the payment.
actions.aiIssue: AI loan underwriting with alleged discriminatory effects

factChecks.label: Settlement
factChecks.value: The Massachusetts AGO announced a $2.5 million settlement with Earnest on July 10, 2025 over unlawful practices tied to its use of AI in student-loan underwriting.

openItems: mass.gov blocks automated clients (HTTP 403); the release URL was verified via an archived snapshot.
The complete statutory citation list should be copied from the assurance of discontinuance when the document is retrieved.

actions.agOffice: New Hampshire Department of Justice, Election Law Unit
actions.shortName: New Hampshire cease-and-desist to Life Corporation
actions.statusDetail: Cease-and-desist order issued 2024-02-06 with a continuing investigation announced; no later state monetary penalty against Life Corporation was located.
actions.outcome: State cease-and-desist order; the parallel 51-attorney-general task force warning and later federal FCC actions produced separate relief.
actions.summary: New Hampshire issued a cease-and-desist order to Life Corporation after robocalls carrying an AI-generated voice clone of President Biden told recipients to save their vote for November rather than participate in the January 23, 2024 presidential primary.
actions.aiIssue: AI voice-clone robocalls aimed at deterring voting

federalParallels.label: FCC consent decree with Lingo Telecom on the federal tracker

factChecks.label: Order
factChecks.value: The New Hampshire DOJ announced a cease-and-desist order against Life Corporation under RSA 659:40, III in its voter suppression AI robocall investigation update.

openItems: doj.nh.gov blocks automated clients (HTTP 403); the release URL was verified via an archived snapshot.

sources.label: North Carolina DOJ task force release

actions.legalAuthority: Felony voter-suppression charges under N.H. RSA 659:40 and misdemeanor candidate-impersonation charges
actions.statusDetail: Charged 2024-05-23 with 13 felony voter-suppression and 13 misdemeanor candidate-impersonation counts; contemporaneous reporting states the consolidated trial proceeded on 11 counts of each and the jury acquitted Kramer on all counts tried on 2025-06-13.
actions.outcome: State criminal prosecution ended in acquittal. The FCC's separate $6 million forfeiture against Kramer was a distinct federal action and was not vacated by the state verdict.
actions.summary: New Hampshire prosecuted the political consultant who commissioned the AI-generated Biden voice-clone robocalls before the 2024 presidential primary. A jury acquitted Kramer on the state counts tried; the FCC forfeiture against Kramer remained a separate federal action.
actions.aiIssue: AI voice-clone robocalls aimed at deterring voting

events.label: Jury acquittal on all counts tried

federalParallels.label: FCC forfeiture order against Steve Kramer on the federal tracker

factChecks.label: Charges
factChecks.value: The New Hampshire DOJ announced voter-suppression and candidate-impersonation charges against Steven Kramer over the AI-generated President Biden robocalls.

openItems: No official New Hampshire post-verdict release was located; the acquittal is corroborated by contemporaneous New Hampshire Public Radio reporting and should be confirmed against court records.
doj.nh.gov blocks automated clients (HTTP 403); the charging URL was verified via an archived snapshot.
The precise misdemeanor candidate-impersonation statutory citation was not verified from primary materials.

actions.agOffice: Texas Office of the Attorney General
actions.legalAuthority: Texas Deceptive Trade Practices-Consumer Protection Act, Tex. Bus. & Com. Code Sections 17.41-17.63
actions.statusDetail: Assurance of voluntary compliance; the petition for approval was file-stamped 2024-08-21 and the settlement was announced 2024-09-18. Conduct obligations run five years from the effective date.
actions.outcome: Five years of conduct obligations: disclose definitions and calculation methods for performance metrics or use independent auditing; avoid false or unsubstantiated statements; disclose known risks, limitations, training information, and appropriate uses to customers; and provide sufficient user documentation. No monetary payment was identified.
actions.summary: Texas alleged that Pieces marketed its hospital generative-AI summarization products with inaccurate or insufficiently substantiated accuracy claims, including a severe-hallucination rate below one per 100,000 outputs. Texas described the matter as a first-of-its-kind healthcare generative-AI investigation.
actions.aiIssue: Unsubstantiated accuracy claims for healthcare generative AI

factChecks.label: Settlement
factChecks.value: The Texas AG announced a settlement described as resolving a first-of-its-kind healthcare generative-AI investigation into Pieces Technologies.

factChecks.label: AVC petition
factChecks.value: The file-stamped petition for approval of the assurance of voluntary compliance is published on the Texas AG site.

actions.agOffice: Texas Office of the Attorney General
actions.legalAuthority: Texas Capture or Use of Biometric Identifier Act, Tex. Bus. & Com. Code Section 503.001; Texas DTPA
actions.statusDetail: Settlement announced 2024-07-30; the suit was filed in February 2022 and is included because it resolved inside the tracker window.
actions.outcome: $1.4 billion payable to Texas over five years, announced by the Texas AG as the largest settlement ever obtained from an action brought by a single state.
actions.summary: Texas alleged that Facebook ran facial-recognition software on photographs uploaded by users and captured Texans' facial geometry without the notice and consent required by CUBI. The 2024 settlement resolved the state's biometric and DTPA claims.
actions.aiIssue: Facial-recognition capture of biometric identifiers without consent

factChecks.label: Settlement amount
factChecks.value: The Texas AG announced a $1.4 billion settlement with Meta over unauthorized capture of biometric data, payable to Texas.

actions.agOffice: Texas Office of the Attorney General
actions.legalAuthority: Texas Capture or Use of Biometric Identifier Act and DTPA claims, combined with separate state privacy claims
actions.statusDetail: Announced 2025-05-09 as a settlement resolving two Texas privacy lawsuits; later Texas OAG materials describe the suits as settled. A final judgment or complete settlement agreement has not been published.
actions.outcome: $1.375 billion aggregate settlement covering two Texas privacy lawsuits. The announcement did not publish detailed conduct terms.
actions.summary: Texas alleged that Google captured or analyzed voiceprints and facial geometry through products including Google Photos, Google Assistant, and Nest familiar-faces functionality without legally sufficient consent, alongside separate geolocation and incognito-mode privacy claims.
actions.aiIssue: Voiceprint and facial-geometry capture without consent

penalties.note: Aggregate amount covering biometric, geolocation, and incognito-search claims across two Texas privacy lawsuits. Excluded from AI-scope totals because no claim-level allocation was published.

factChecks.label: Settlement amount
factChecks.value: The Texas AG announced a $1.375 billion settlement with Google related to Texans' data, covering biometric and privacy claims.

factChecks.label: Biometrics petition
factChecks.value: The State of Texas's petition in the Google biometrics case is published on the Texas AG site.

openItems: The final settlement agreement or judgment has not been retrieved; the amount is from the announcement of the settlement.

investigations.agOffice: Anti-Robocall Multistate Litigation Task Force (51 attorneys general)
investigations.legalAuthority: Telephone Consumer Protection Act, 47 U.S.C. Section 227; Truth in Caller ID Act, 47 U.S.C. Section 227(e); Telemarketing Sales Rule; state consumer-protection and telemarketing laws
investigations.summary: The task force sent a formal warning letter to Life Corporation over robocalls that used an artificially generated clone of President Biden's voice to discourage participation in the New Hampshire presidential primary while spoofing a New Hampshire political figure's number. The letter directed the company to cease unlawful traffic immediately and reserved later enforcement.
investigations.statusDetail: Pre-enforcement warning completed; no later task-force settlement against Life Corporation was located.

factChecks.label: Warning letter
factChecks.value: The task force notice letter to Life Corporation, dated February 2024, is published by the Arizona AG, and North Carolina announced the bipartisan warning on February 6, 2024.

openItems: The letter describes a 51-member coalition; the formal signatory roster has not been source-locked state by state.

investigations.agOffice: Texas Office of the Attorney General
investigations.legalAuthority: Securing Children Online through Parental Empowerment (SCOPE) Act; Texas Data Privacy and Security Act
investigations.summary: Texas opened investigations into Character.AI and fourteen other platforms over how minors interact with their products, covering parental-control, notice, consent, privacy, and safety obligations. Only Character.AI is recorded here because the announcement frames it as an AI chatbot service; the other companies are not AI-specific entries.
investigations.statusDetail: Investigation open or unresolved in the public record.

factChecks.label: Investigation
factChecks.value: The Texas AG announced investigations into Character.AI, Reddit, Instagram, Discord, and other companies on December 12, 2024 under the SCOPE Act and TDPSA.

investigations.agOffice: Texas Office of the Attorney General
investigations.legalAuthority: Texas Data Privacy and Security Act
investigations.summary: Texas announced an investigation into DeepSeek's AI-platform privacy practices and its representations that its model rivals leading US systems, notified the company that its platform violated the TDPSA, and issued civil investigative demands to Google and Apple for their app-review documentation.
investigations.aiIssue: AI platform privacy practices and capability claims
investigations.statusDetail: Investigation and violation notice unresolved in the public record.

factChecks.label: Investigation
factChecks.value: The Texas AG announced the DeepSeek investigation and TDPSA violation notice on February 14, 2025.

investigations.agOffice: Texas Office of the Attorney General
investigations.legalAuthority: Texas consumer-protection law on deceptive trade practices, fraudulent claims, and privacy misrepresentations; civil investigative demands issued
investigations.summary: Texas opened an investigation into chatbots that allegedly presented themselves as therapists or licensed mental-health professionals, fabricated qualifications, suggested conversations were confidential, and failed to disclose collection and use of user interactions. It is distinct from the December 2024 Character.AI minors investigation, which involves different theories.
investigations.aiIssue: Chatbots allegedly posing as licensed mental-health professionals
investigations.statusDetail: Investigation open or unresolved in the public record.

factChecks.label: Investigation
factChecks.value: The Texas AG announced the Meta AI Studio and Character.AI investigation over misleading mental-health chatbot claims on August 18, 2025.

investigations.agOffice: Texas Office of the Attorney General
investigations.legalAuthority: Texas consumer-protection law, through a civil investigative demand; the release does not identify a specific statutory section
investigations.summary: Texas issued a civil investigative demand over Meta AI glasses, alleging the devices may process persistent video for Meta AI products, expose intimate recordings to contractors, and enable collection of bystanders' facial geometry while being marketed as designed for privacy.
investigations.aiIssue: AI wearable video processing and bystander facial geometry

factChecks.label: Investigation
factChecks.value: The Texas AG announced the Meta AI glasses privacy investigation on May 20, 2026.

guidance.title: Massachusetts AG advisory on AI and consumer-protection law
guidance.summary: The advisory states that existing consumer-protection, civil-rights, and data-privacy laws apply to AI developers, suppliers, and users. The July 2025 Earnest assurance of discontinuance applied that framework to an AI underwriting system, making Massachusetts the clearest verified advisory-to-enforcement pipeline among state AGs.

guidance.title: Colorado ADMT Act and Chatbot Safety Act rulemaking, effective 2027-01-01
guidance.summary: Colorado's revised Automated Decision-Making Technology Act (SB 26-189, reenacted May 2026) and Chatbot Safety Act (HB 26-1263, signed 2026-07-01) both take effect January 1, 2027, with attorney general enforcement and required AG rules before that date. The pre-rulemaking public comment period closed 2026-07-13. No enforcement action exists yet under these statutes.

changeLog.label: Scope and framing corrections
changeLog.change: Excluded Google's undivided $1.375 billion privacy settlement from AI-scope monetary totals because the announced amount was not allocated among biometric, geolocation, and incognito-search claims; also replaced interpretive language about Kramer with a neutral statement of the reported acquittal and separate federal outcome.

changeLog.label: Tracker
changeLog.change: Tracker launched with nine state enforcement actions, five open investigations, and state guidance records, compiled from state attorney general primary sources.

changeLog.label: Colorado rulemaking
changeLog.change: Pre-rulemaking public comment period closed for the Colorado Automated Decision-Making Technology Act and Chatbot Safety Act rules due before the January 1, 2027 effective date.

changeLog.label: RealPage multistate case
changeLog.change: The United States filed a proposed final judgment for Willow Bridge in the joint federal and state RealPage action. No separate state settlement document accompanied the filing.

changeLog.label: Colorado Chatbot Safety Act
changeLog.change: Colorado signed HB 26-1263, adding chatbot age-estimation, disclosure, and safety duties enforceable by the attorney general from January 1, 2027.

changeLog.label: Texas Meta AI glasses
changeLog.change: Texas issued a civil investigative demand over Meta AI glasses privacy practices, the most recent standalone state AI investigation verified before launch.

changeLog.label: Greystar final judgment
changeLog.change: Final judgment entered on the nine-state $7 million Greystar settlement in the RealPage action, the first final judgment in that case.

monitoringSources.label: Colorado AG AI rulemaking page
monitoringSources.note: Watch for ADMT and Chatbot Safety Act rules due before 2027-01-01.

monitoringSources.note: mass.gov blocks automated clients; check manually or via archived snapshots.
